body: We moved to Clapham last year with our two Bengals and spent three weeks researching vets. The Stitch recommendation above is solid — going in person first was the thing that helped most.
Same experience in Hackney. The first vet we tried had a 3-week wait for non-urgent, which sounds fine until your dog eats something random.

The RCVS accreditation point is underrated. I didn't know about the Practice Standards Scheme until I started reading this stuff. Our current vet is a RCVS Hospital — they have an on-site lab and everything.
Can anyone recommend a vet in the Lewisham/Forest Hill area? Just adopted a 2-year-old lurcher and we're completely new to this.
Try Forest Hill Vets on Dartmouth Road — we've been there for 4 years with our two dogs. Honest pricing, never felt like they were upselling.
Worth noting: the emergency clinic thing is a real expense to factor in. Our regular vet refers to Vets Now which charges a £160 consult fee just to walk in. Found that out the hard way.
This is genuinely useful to know. I had no idea some practices don't cover their own out-of-hours.
The time-limited vs lifetime distinction tripped us up badly. Had a policy that seemed comprehensive, dog was diagnosed with IBD at age 5, and by age 6 it was fully excluded. Now we self-insure.
This is exactly why I always tell people: get lifetime cover from day one, not when they're already showing issues.
The co-payment maths in this article is something policies really bury. 20% + £250 excess sounds manageable until you work it out on a £4,000 bill. That's £1,050 you're paying yourself.
I have Bought By Many (now ManyPets) and genuinely rate them. The dental cover extra is worth it for my Persian — she's had two dental procedures in two years.
How much is the dental extra roughly? My Ragdoll is 3 and I've been putting off sorting cover.
The database issue is real and so underreported. My neighbour's cat was missing for 3 weeks, found, chipped — but the registration had an old email address. Took another week to sort.
Is there a free way to check which database your chip is registered on? Our cat is from a rescue 2 years ago and I've never verified it.
Check-a-chip.co.uk — free, just enter the 15-digit number from your chip card. Any vet can scan the chip for free in seconds if you don't have the number.
The nail/floor clicking thing is such a good indicator. My Cavapoo started doing this and I was embarrassed I hadn't noticed. Four weeks post-groomer and she walks completely differently.

The matting section is so important. My partner thought we could just cut out the mat at home with scissors and ended up cutting the dog's skin. Required a vet visit. Please read this point carefully.
Can anyone recommend a groomer in Peckham/East Dulwich? Preferably someone who does Cockapoos and doesn't shave them completely (the 'puppy cut' look is not it for us).

Finally — a balanced take. The raw feeding forums get so aggressive toward anyone who feeds kibble. My Labrador has been on Hill's Science Plan for 8 years and is in excellent health. There's no universal answer.
Agreed. Though I do feel like the kibble-is-fine crowd sometimes dismisses the ingredient quality issue a bit quickly. There's a meaningful difference between Pro Plan and Iceland own-brand.

The zoonotic risk point is real and under-discussed, especially in households with kids. We switched to Natures Menu raw and I'm genuinely meticulous about the prep. Probably fine — but it's a consideration.
We followed almost exactly this plan with our rescue greyhound. Key thing we learned: step 5 (closing the door) really can't be rushed. We spent 3 days on that step alone.
This. Everyone says '7-day plan' like it's guaranteed to take 7 days. With our Romanian rescue it was closer to 3 weeks. But it worked, and now she goes in voluntarily to nap.
The tip about removing the door entirely at first is something I hadn't seen before. That was genuinely useful — our boy was suspicious of the hinge mechanism itself.

The PDSA point deserves to be at the top. So many people don't know it exists. Our dog needed emergency surgery last year — £0 cost because we qualify for Universal Credit. Would have been over £4,000 otherwise.
This genuinely made me cry. Sharing this article because people need to know. The PDSA Hammersmith centre is incredible.
The crowdfunding point is real. When my cat needed a £2,800 operation last year I posted on a local Facebook group almost as a last resort. Raised £1,400 in two days. People genuinely want to help.
The 10-day window on the AHC catches people out. We nearly missed our ferry because I thought we needed it a month in advance. Booked the OV appointment for day 9 before travel and it was fine.

The tapeworm treatment for re-entry is not on most people's radar at all. First time we brought our dog back from Spain we had no idea — a very kind border officer flagged it rather than turned us away.
The OV vet cost is real. I called 5 local vets and only two of them were OVs and willing to do the AHC. Booked weeks in advance — it's not a drop-in service.
